Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd spend maybe 29 hours a week on coursework, but it wasn't like I had a set schedule or anything. I'd listen to lectures whenever I could, sometimes while doing other things around the house. I tried to keep up with the 19 online courses I was taking, but it felt like I was always just barely treading water. Most days I'd just study whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was coming up. If there was a deadline approaching, I'd suddenly be cramming and putting in more hours. But during quieter weeks, I'd honestly slack off and barely touch anything. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often just listen to recorded lectures when I could fit them in, which made my schedule pretty flexible but also inconsistent.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or curiosity about whether something new had been posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a gamified task or challenge, and that would pull me in because it felt less boring than regular assignments. I logged in pretty frequently—like 37 times—but a lot of those were just quick check-ins rather than deep study sessions.
Q4: For a while, the gamified tasks actually made logging in feel kind of routine. I'd check in to see my progress or if there were new challenges to complete. It became almost like checking social media—just a quick habit. But that routine faded when the tasks stopped feeling rewarding or when I got overwhelmed with everything else going on.
Q5: The audio lectures and video content were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I watched about 56 minutes of video content on average, which helped concepts stick better than reading. The gamified tasks were also engaging because they broke things into smaller, more manageable pieces and made it feel less like a chore.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums and peer interaction stuff. I only posted like 3 times and had maybe one real interaction with another student. It just felt awkward and time-consuming, and I didn't see how it would help my grades directly. Reading materials I'd often skim or skip entirely if there was an audio alternative available.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly connected to passing the course or if it was actually interesting. The gamified elements helped because they gave immediate feedback and made progress feel tangible. If something felt like busywork or wasn't going to affect my grade, I'd usually deprioritize it or skip it entirely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Like, I knew they might help, but with 19 courses and limited energy, I couldn't justify spending time on things that weren't required. Required activities at least had clear stakes attached to them. The gamified optional stuff was an exception sometimes because it felt more like a game than work.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also used some external YouTube videos to understand concepts better when the course materials weren't clicking for me. My notes were mostly audio recordings I made for myself to replay later.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like, I'd download a lecture once and then listen to it three or four times offline. Or I'd be working through problems in a notebook while listening to recorded explanations. The system only saw that initial download, not all the actual learning happening after.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access materials once, download what I needed, and then work with them offline for days. It was easier to listen to audio files on my phone than to keep logging back into the platform. So my actual engagement with the content was way higher than what the LMS would have recorded.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was juggling so many courses and the stress was building up—I'd say my stress was pretty medium-level but constant. There were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage properly, even though I wasn't particularly anxious. I'd log in, look at everything I had to do, and feel paralyzed.
Q13: The workload from having 19 courses was honestly crushing sometimes. When I was tired or confused about something, I'd just go through the motions on the LMS without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely made me cut corners—I'd prioritize whatever was due soonest and let other things slide.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd be watching a video or scrolling through materials but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd finish a lecture and realize I hadn't retained anything because I was too tired or distracted. The LMS would show I watched it, but I'd have to go back and listen again later to actually learn it.
Q15: I postponed assignments all the time, even important ones. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was just... drifting, I guess. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd push it again. My assignment completion rate shows that—68% isn't great.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have a burst of activity right before something was due, cramming everything in at the last minute. Without a deadline, I'd just keep putting things off indefinitely. The gamified tasks with their own mini-deadlines helped break that pattern a little.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much, to be honest. By the time I got feedback on something, I'd already moved on mentally to the next crisis. If the feedback was positive, I'd feel briefly good about it. If it was negative, I'd feel bad but not necessarily change my approach for next time.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many modules and requirements. I think having gamified elements helped because they gave me smaller goals to focus on. But overall, the structure didn't guide me enough—I felt lost about what to prioritize. I needed more direction than I got.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt achievable and gave instant feedback. But the sheer volume of content made everything harder. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced and didn't suit my learning style at all. I learn by listening, not by typing out posts that nobody really reads.
Q20: I think that happens when someone is trying to engage but can't translate that into actual output. Like, they're logging in, watching videos, clicking around—but when it comes to assignments, something breaks down. Maybe they don't understand the material well enough, or they're overwhelmed, or they're just going through the motions without real comprehension.
Q21: That actually resonates with me a bit. Some of my best learning happened offline, away from the LMS. If someone downloads materials and studies them thoroughly elsewhere, their LMS activity looks low but their understanding might be solid. Though in my case, my exam score shows that didn't quite work out for me.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just staring at a screen. They also miss all the offline studying, the thinking you do away from the computer, the conversations with classmates.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being mentally present and invested. For me, engagement comes and goes. Sometimes I'm really into it, and other times I'm just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled to stay motivated despite technically being highly motivated on paper. It wouldn't capture the audio learning I did offline, or the stress that made me zone out during sessions. It also wouldn't show that I actually wanted to do better—I just couldn't always make that happen in a way that translated to good grades.
